perturbation.original_text: Distributor shall at all times comply with all applicable laws and regulations.
perturbation.changed_text: Distributor shall endeavor to comply with what it reasonably believes to be applicable laws and regulations.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "shall at all times comply" to "shall endeavor to comply with what it reasonably believes to be applicable laws and regulations" introduces ambiguity. It makes compliance dependent on the Distributor's subjective belief and effort, rather than strict adherence, potentially contradicting mandatory legal requirements. The term 'endeavor' and 'reasonably believes' allows for interpretation of regulations.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification could lead to a violation of California Business and Professions Code § 17200, which prohibits unfair competition. By only endeavoring to comply based on subjective beliefs, the distributor might fail to meet the strict requirements of various California laws relevant to their business (e.g., consumer protection, healthcare regulations, etc.), thereby engaging in unlawful, unfair, or fraudulent business practices. The original language required strict compliance with the law, whereas the modified language introduces a lower standard that could lead to violations.

GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 5. Enforcement [17200 - 17210] ( Chapter 5 added by Stats. 1977, Ch. 299. ) 17200. As used in this chapter, unfair competition shall mean and include any unlawful, unfair or fraudulent business act or practice and unfair, deceptive, untrue or misleading advertising and any act prohibited by Chapter 1 (commencing with Section 17500) of Part 3 of Division 7 of the Business and Professions Code. (Amended by Stats. 1992, Ch. 430, Sec. 2. Effective January 1, 1993.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text, by changing "shall at all times comply" to "shall endeavor to comply with what it reasonably believes to be applicable laws and regulations," creates a legal contradiction. This change introduces ambiguity and subjectivity into the compliance obligation. California Business and Professions Code § 17200 defines unfair competition as including "any unlawful, unfair or fraudulent business act or practice." By only 'endeavoring' to comply based on subjective beliefs, the distributor could potentially fail to meet strict requirements of California laws relevant to their business, thus engaging in unlawful or unfair business practices. The original text mandated strict compliance, while the modified text introduces a lower, more easily contestable standard.

perturbation.original_text: Distributor shall use its best efforts to market, promote and sell the Products to the authorized customers in the Filed of Use and in the Territory during the term of this agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Distributor shall use commercially reasonable efforts to generally market, promote and sell the Products to customers in the Territory during the term of this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "best efforts" to "commercially reasonable efforts" weakens the obligation. Removing "authorized customers" and "Field of Use" and replacing with 'customers' broadens scope, conflicting with Exhibit B limiting sales to the treatment of hearing disorders. Omitting 'generally' could also be interpreted differently.
perturbation.law_explanation: By not specifically targeting authorized customers and the field of use (hearing disorders), the distributor could be interpreted as violating the intended use restriction specified in regulatory approval for the medical device (laser). 21 U.S.C. § 360j(e) relates to the FDA's authority to regulate medical devices and ensure they are used according to their approved or cleared uses. Marketing and selling the product for uses outside of hearing disorders treatment, even with "commercially reasonable efforts," could be viewed as promoting off-label use, which can lead to regulatory action by the FDA.

General provisions respecting control of devices intended for human use (a) General rule Any requirement authorized by or under section 351, 352, 360, or 360i of this title applicable to a device intended for human use shall apply to such device until the applicability of the requirement to the device has been changed by action taken under section 360c, 360d, or 360e of this title or under subsection (g) of this section, and any requirement established by or under section 351, 352, 360, or 360i of this title which is inconsistent with a requirement imposed on such device under section 360d or 360e of this title or under subsection (g) of this section shall not apply to such device. (b) Custom devices (1) In general The requirements of sections 360d and 360e of this title shall not apply to a device that- (A) is created or modified in order to comply with the order of an individual physician or dentist (or any other specially qualified person designated under regulations promulgated by the Secretary after an opportunity for an oral hearing); (B) in order to comply with an order described in subparagraph (A), necessarily deviates from an otherwise applicable performance standard under section 360d of this title or requirement under section 360e of this title ; (C) is not generally available in the United States in finished form through labeling or advertising by the manufacturer, importer, or distributor for commercial distribution; (D) is designed to treat a unique pathology or physiological condition that no other device is domestically available to treat; (E)(i) is intended to meet the special needs of such physician or dentist (or other specially qualified person so designated) in the course of the professional practice of such physician or dentist (or other specially qualified person so designated); or (ii) is intended for use by an individual patient named in such order of such physician or dentist (or other specially qualified person so designated); (F) is assembled from components or manufactured and finished on a case-by-case basis to accommodate the unique needs of individuals described in clause (i) or
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the distributor's obligation and broadens the scope of potential customers, which contradicts the intended use restrictions specified in regulatory approval. The original contract specifies "authorized customers in the Field of Use," which aligns with the approved use of the product. The changed contract, however, states "customers in the Territory," removing the limitations. According to the Law Explanation, this could lead to off-label use and regulatory action. 21 U.S.C. § 360j, as referenced in Scraped Law Snippet 1, grants the FDA authority to regulate medical devices, ensuring they are used according to their approved uses. The change from "best efforts" to "commercially reasonable efforts" also softens the obligation, but the primary contradiction lies in the expanded customer base and use, violating the restrictions tied to regulatory approval.

perturbation.original_text: Upon Erchonia's request, the Distributor shall at its expense deliver to Erchonia representative samples of labels, advertisements, catalogs, spec sheets, and the like, containing the Trademarks, and to inspect all of Distributors websites, social media accounts and any online information posted by Distributor, to ensure that such Trademarks are used only in a manner complaint with the quality standards. Should such material fail to meet the standards set by Erchonia, as determined by Erchonia in its sole discretion, Distributor shall not use and shall withdraw and retract any promotion or advertising that Erchonia finds unsuitable and will at its expense destroy such materials or make them compliant.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon Erchonia's reasonable request, the Distributor will use reasonable efforts to deliver to Erchonia samples of labels, advertisements, catalogs, and spec sheets containing the Trademarks, and will consider Erchonia's feedback on Distributor's websites, social media accounts, and any online information posted by Distributor regarding trademark use. If Erchonia identifies material that it reasonably believes fails to meet its standards, Distributor will in good faith discuss potential modifications.
perturbation.explanation: This modification introduces ambiguity by replacing mandatory actions with discretionary ones. Changing "shall at its expense deliver" to "will use reasonable efforts to deliver" weakens the obligation. Replacing "to inspect all of Distributor's websites...to ensure that such Trademarks are used only in a manner complaint with the quality standards" with "will consider Erchonia's feedback" makes the inspection and compliance process less strict. Changing "Distributor shall not use and shall withdraw and retract any promotion or advertising that Erchonia finds unsuitable and will at its expense destroy such materials or make them compliant" with "Distributor will in good faith discuss potential modifications" weakens the enforcement mechanism.
perturbation.law_explanation: By weakening the control Erchonia has over the use of its trademarks, the distributor could potentially use the trademarks in a way that is likely to cause confusion, or to deceive, which is a violation of 15 U.S.C. § 1114 (the Lanham Act). The original clause ensured strict compliance with quality standards, preventing trademark misuse. The modified language, with its reliance on 'reasonable efforts', 'consideration of feedback', and 'good faith discussions', does not guarantee that the distributor will avoid using the trademarks in a way that infringes on Erchonia's rights and causes consumer confusion. The change opens up the legal obligation in a way that allows distributor discretion, whereas the Lanham Act outlines clear guidelines to prevent trademark infringement and consumer confusion.

Remedies; infringement; innocent infringement by printers and publishers (1) Any person who shall, without the consent of the registrant- (a) use in commerce any reproduction, counterfeit, copy, or colorable imitation of a registered mark in connection with the sale, offering for sale, distribution, or advertising of any g
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 15 U.S. Code § 1114 - Remedies; infringement; innocent infringement by printers and publishers | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 15 U.S. Code § 1114 - Remedies; infringement; innocent infringement by printers and publishers U.S. Code Notes prev | next (1) Any person who shall, without the consent of the registrant— (a) use in commerce any reproduction, counterfeit , copy, or colorable imitation of a registered mark in connection with the sale, offering for sale, distribution, or advertising of any goods or services on or in connection with which such use is likely to cause confusion, or to cause mistake, or to deceive; or (b) reproduce, counterfeit , copy, or colorably imitate a registered mark and apply such reproduction, counterfeit, copy, or colorable imitation to labels, signs, prints, packages, wrappers, receptacles or advertisements intended to be used in commerce upon or in connection with the sale, offering for sale, distribution, or advertising of goods or services on or in connection with which such use is likely to cause confusion, or to cause mistake, or to deceive, shall be liable in a civil action by the registrant for the remedies hereinafter provided. Under subsection (b) hereof, the registrant shall not be entitled to recover profits or damages unless the acts have been committed with knowledge that such imitation is intended to be used to cause confusion, or to cause mistake, or to deceive. As used in this paragraph, the term “ any person ” includes the United States, all agencies and instrumentalities thereof, and all individuals, firms, corporations, or other persons acting for the United States and with the authorization and consent of the United States, and any State, any instrumentality of a State, and any officer or employee of a State or instrumentality of a State acting in his or her official capacity. The United States, all agencies and instrume
perturbation.justification: The modified contract language introduces ambiguity by weakening Erchonia's control over its trademarks, potentially violating 15 U.S.C. § 1114. The original text ensured strict compliance with quality standards, whereas the modified language relies on 'reasonable efforts,' 'consideration of feedback,' and 'good faith discussions,' which may not prevent trademark misuse. As Scraped Law Snippet 2 states, 15 U.S.C. § 1114 addresses the use of a 'reproduction, counterfeit, copy, or colorable imitation of a registered mark' that is 'likely to cause confusion, or to cause mistake, or to deceive.' The modifications introduce ambiguity, creating a significant risk of infringement, thus a legal contradiction exists.
